metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = refocused echo / realigned spins / return of signal; CRIMSON = dephased / fanned-out spins / lost signal
metadata.note: No Bloch-equation derivation, no T1/T2 formalism, no rotating-frame algebra.

beats.narration_text: Put a million spins in a magnetic field. They all start pointing the same direction. Wait a moment — they fan out into a smeared mess and their combined signal vanishes.
Apply one carefully timed pulse. The fan reverses. At exactly twice the wait, every spin snaps back into step — and the signal returns from nothing. That is the spin echo.
How does one flip pulse undo what randomness started — and why does the echo arrive at exactly twice the wait time?

props.headline: How does one flip pulse undo what randomness started — and…

card.headline:
Spins fan out, signal dies.
One π-pulse: signal returns at 2τ.
card.dek: The spin echo: time-reversing inhomogeneous dephasing

beats.narration_text: In a real magnet the field is not perfectly uniform. Each spin sees a slightly different field and precesses at a slightly different rate — faster or slower than the mean.
The fast spins run ahead; the slow ones fall behind. After time tau the fan has spread over 360 degrees. The total magnetization averages to zero — the signal dies.
A pi-pulse flips every spin — it mirrors them across the equator. The spin that was 30 degrees ahead is now 30 degrees behind. The one that was behind is now ahead. Their ordering is reversed.
After the flip, the fast spins are still fast — but they are now behind. They chase the slow ones. After exactly another tau, everyone arrives at the same point. Echo.
The echo amplitude decays as e to the minus 2 tau over T2 — only from real spin-spin interactions that are not time-reversible. Static field errors cancel exactly.
This is why MRI works in an imperfect magnet. Vary tau, measure echo heights, fit the decay — you get T2 from a machine whose field is uneven by one part in a million.
Illustrative: tau equals 50 ms. Echo amplitudes at 50, 100, 200 ms are 0.85, 0.72, 0.52 of maximum. Fitting e to the minus 2 tau over T2 gives T2 approximately 360 milliseconds.
The spins dispersed because they precessed at different rates. A pi-pulse reversed who was ahead. After another wait the same length, they realigned. Time reversed — at 2 tau.

props.headline: The spins dispersed because they precessed at different rates. A pi-pulse…

card.headline:
Echo at t = 2τ.
Amplitude: e^(−2τ/T₂).
card.dek: The π-pulse exchanges fast and slow spins. Inhomogeneous dephasing is reversible; real T2 decay is not.

beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Approximation Methods and Real Systems series.
